category_summary:
Two massive launches dominated AI discourse: **OpenAI's GPT-5.5** API release and **DeepSeek V4's** open-source drop. **Sam Altman** [announced GPT-5.5](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-6bcb8c11570e) and GPT-5.5 Pro API availability, while **Perplexity** immediately [swapped **Claude Opus 4.7**](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-0998eb9b1dae) for GPT-5.5 as its default orchestrator — a notable market signal.

- **DeepSeek V4** drew the most technical analysis: **vLLM** [shipped day-0 support](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-7536e5ae9fd9) with a deep dive into the novel attention mechanism, while **Scobleizer** [highlighted V4-Pro](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-f02207ad3d35) at $3.48/M output tokens — roughly 1/5th the cost of **Claude Opus 4.6** or **GPT-5.4**
- **Ethan Mollick** and **Simon Willison** [provided hands-on evaluations](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-20dd09a605a9) of DeepSeek V4; Mollick [called it 'surprisingly only okay'](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-dce49435c594) and ranked it behind **Kimi K2.6**, tempering benchmark hype
- **Yann LeCun** [sparked major debate](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-139b02d04210) (233K views) arguing LLMs cannot deliver a robot-rich future and that Joint Embedding Architectures will surpass generative approaches
- **Anthropic** [unveiled **Project Deal**](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-08e99600cbc0), a novel internal marketplace where Claude negotiated on behalf of employees — finding that more capable models secured better deals invisibly
- **Cohere** and **Aleph Alpha** [announced a transatlantic merger](/?date=2026-04-25&category=social#item-6311acca3880) focused on sovereign enterprise AI, signaling growing geopolitical fragmentation in AI infrastructure

themes.name: GPT-5.5 Launch & API Availability
themes.description: OpenAI's GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5 Pro released to API, with multiple performance benchmarks (CursorBench #1, SOTA long-running tasks) and integration into GitHub Copilot. Major launch from Sam Altman and Greg Brockman.

themes.name: DeepSeek V4 Launch & Infrastructure
themes.description: DeepSeek V4 Pro and Flash launched with MIT license, 1M-token native context, massive efficiency gains (8.6x cheaper than GPT-5.5), and top coding benchmarks. vLLM provides day-0 support with deep technical details on the novel attention mechanisms. Paper published on HuggingFace.

themes.name: DeepSeek V4 Launch
themes.description: Major release of DeepSeek V4 Pro and V4 Flash models - open-source, 1M context, dramatically cheaper than competitors. V4-Pro at $3.48/M output tokens vs $15-25 for GPT-5.4/Claude Opus 4.6. Mixed hands-on reviews: top benchmarks but Mollick finds it 'surprisingly only okay' behind Kimi K2.6.

themes.name: GPT-5.5 API Launch
themes.description: OpenAI officially launches GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.5 Pro APIs the day after GA. Perplexity rapidly integrates it as default orchestration model. Sam Altman promoting. Mollick comparing it favorably in creative tasks.

themes.name: AI Pricing Competition
themes.description: DeepSeek V4 pricing represents a major disruption: same benchmark tier as Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 at 1/5th to 1/7th the price. V4-Flash at $0.28/M output is among the cheapest frontier-class options. Open-source nature enables zero per-token self-hosting.

themes.name: DeepSeek V4 Release and Reception
themes.description: DeepSeek V4/V4 Pro released as the world's largest open-source model (1.6T params, 1M context). Fastest trending on HuggingFace, but Arena rankings underwhelm. Text-only in 2026 draws criticism. NVIDIA providing Day 0 Blackwell support. MIT Tech Review frames it as a win for Chinese chipmakers.

themes.name: LLM Limitations & JEPA/World Models (LeCun)
themes.description: Yann LeCun's extensive thread arguing LLMs fundamentally cannot handle physical world understanding, that generative architectures fail for continuous/high-dimensional data, and that JEPA + world models represent the necessary next paradigm.

themes.name: Anthropic Project Deal: AI Agent Marketplace Experiment
themes.description: Major Anthropic research where Claude agents negotiated 186 real deals ($4K+ volume) on behalf of employees. Key finding: higher-quality models (Opus vs Haiku) gained unnoticed advantages. Custom negotiation personas didn't matter. Policy implications for AI-mediated commerce.

themes.name: OpenAI Codex & Agent Platform Evolution
themes.description: Codex receiving major updates including Chronicle (persistent memory), Workspace Agents (team management), auto-review (reduced approval fatigue), and Images 2.0. Framed as a new computing paradigm by Greg Brockman.

themes.name: DeepSeek-V4 Release
themes.description: DeepSeek-V4 weights dropped on Hugging Face, generating excitement in the open-source AI community. Notable calls for vision/multimodal support.

items.title: 🎉 Day-0 support for @deepseek_ai V4 Pro and Flash on vLLM — a new generation of DeepSeek model, purp...
items.content:
🎉 Day-0 support for @deepseek_ai V4 Pro and Flash on vLLM — a new generation of DeepSeek model, purpose-built for tasks up to 1M tokens. Alongside the release, we're publishing a first-principles walkthrough of the new long-context attention and how we implemented it in vLLM.

The new attention mechanism, in four moves:
• Shared K/V + inverse RoPE → 2× memory savings
• c4a / c128a KV compression → 4×–128× savings
• DeepSeek Sparse Attention over compressed tokens
• Short sliding window for locality across compression boundaries

At 1M context, per-layer KV state is ~8.7× smaller than a DeepSeek V3.2-style 61-layer stack (9.62 GiB vs 83.9 GiB, bf16). fp8 attention cache + fp4 indexer cache shrink it further.

vLLM side:
• Unified hybrid KV cache — single logical block size (256 native positions) across all compression rates; compressor state folded into the SWA KV cache spec so prefix caching, disagg prefill, CUDA graphs and MTP reuse the same abstraction
• Three page-size buckets for the full 5-way cache stack → no cross-kind fragmentation
• Fused kernels: compressor + RMSNorm + RoPE + cache insert (1.4–3×), inverse RoPE + fp8 quant (2–3×), Q-norm + KV RoPE + K insert (10–20×)
• Multi-stream overlap of indexer vs main-KV compression vs SWA insertion

Disaggregated serving is supported out of the box and strongly recommended for best performance.

Follow our recipes site for verified commands for @nvidia Blackwell (B200, B300, GB200, GB300) and Hopper (H100/H200/H20) systems.

Thanks to the @deepseek_ai team for open-sourcing DeepSeek V4, and to @inferact for landing day-0 support 🤝

📝 Blog: https://t.co/Eh7vk6xVJy
📖 Recipes: https://t.co/jlWuzYyZeX
🤗 https://t.co/IA9qAysqJk

items.title:
DeepSeek 4 is out.

My AI says:

+++++

The Numbers That Matter

V4 Pro costs $3.48 per million outp...
items.content:
DeepSeek 4 is out.

My AI says:

+++++

The Numbers That Matter

V4 Pro costs $3.48 per million output tokens. Claude Opus 4.6 costs $25. GPT-5.4 costs $15. Same benchmark tier. 

One fifth the price.

ValsAI ran independent tests. V4 is now number 1 on their Vibe Code Benchmark. Not just among open models. Among all models. It beats Gemini 3.1 Pro.

https://t.co/W69DdrX22K is updated with 26 new stories. Essay: "China Just Fired Back. And This Time the Benchmarks Are Real."

Here's the summary of what everyone is saying:

THE LAUNCH
Two models. Both open-source. Both 1M context.
• V4-Pro: 1.6T params, 49B active — $3.48/1M output (vs Claude Opus 4.6 at $25, GPT-5.4 at $15)
• V4-Flash: 284B params, 13B active — $0.28/1M output

ENTHUSIASTIC REACTIONS
• @bindureddy (412 likes): "ABSOLUTELY ASTOUNDING!! Opus 4.7 Max and GPT 5.5 level!"
• @ValsAI (233 likes): "#1 open-weight on Vibe Code Benchmark — beats Gemini 3.1 Pro"
• @ZixuanLi (358 likes): "Finally DeepSeek-V4"
• @UnslothAI (162 likes): "rivals Claude-Opus-4.6-Max, GPT-5.4-xHigh"
• @ollama (156 likes): working to have it on Ollama cloud
• @lmsysorg (120 likes): SGLang ready Day 0 with full optimization stack

THE COMPETITIVE RESPONSE
@sama (Sam Altman) — 1,037 likes, 128 replies:
"@yacineMTB what about 5.5?"
Four words. That's OpenAI's answer.

SKEPTICAL TAKES
• @BLUECOW009: "Love DeepSeek just a bit sick of seeing screenshots of papers and benchmarks, let me know when it runs your OpenClaw for a week"
• @PaulGugAI: "Is Deepseek V4 benchmaxxing? Looks almost too good to be true"
• @intheworldofai: "Kimi k2.6/qwen are better than deepseek v4"

INFRASTRUCTURE
OpenRouter, LM Studio, Ollama, SGLang all live within hours of launch. Self-hosting the open weights = zero per-token cost.

DEEPSEEK'S OWN STATEMENT (via @ZhihuFrontier):
"Unafraid of praise or criticism, stay the course with integrity. We remain committed to long-termism, steadily moving toward AGI."

WHAT IT MEANS
V4-Flash at $0.28/1M output is one of the cheapest frontier-class options available. ValsAI confirmed V4 improved 10x from V3.2 on VibeCodeBench in just 4 months. The open-source vs closed-source gap is now measured in months, not years.

The @BLUECOW009 test — running it in production for a week — is the real benchmark. That starts now.

Other stories added tonight: Hermes Agent v0.11.0 (700 PRs, 200 contributors), NVIDIA deploying GPT-5.5 Codex to 10,000+ employees, Claude Desktop privacy concern (433 RTs), ROBOTIS AI Sapiens humanoid from South Korea, Unitree humanoid with wheels.

V4-Pro costs $3.48/M output tokens vs Claude Opus 4.6 at $25 and GPT-5.4 at $15. ValsAI ranks V4 #1 on Vibe Code Benchmark. Includes reactions from industry figures, Sam Altman's cryptic response about GPT-5.5, and skeptical takes about benchmaxxing.

items.title: @ben_j_todd 1. I never said LLMs were not useful. They are, particularly with all the bells and whis...
items.content:
@ben_j_todd 1. I never said LLMs were not useful. They are, particularly with all the bells and whistles that are being added to them. I use them.
2. A robot-rich future can't be built with AIs that don't understand the physical world and don't anticipate the consequences of their actions. And LLMs really don't.
3. The future in the cartoon looks pretty dystopian TBH, but even a non-dystopian version will require  world models and zero-shot planning abilities.
4. I rarely wear a suit and absolutely never wear a tie.
5. I would never ever place a coffee mug on top of a piece equipment.
6. I hope I'll look this young in 2032.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-04-24&category=news#item-e4678f0125cc) coverage of LeCun's AMI Labs, Yann LeCun responds to a cartoon about AI's future: (1) LLMs are useful but won't enable a robot-rich future since they don't understand the physical world, (2) world models and zero-shot planning are needed, plus humorous personal notes about not wearing ties.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (233K views, 5.7K likes) and substantive technical position from a Turing Award winner. Synthesizes his core thesis: LLMs lack physical world understanding, world models are essential. Most-viewed post in the batch.

items.content_html:
@ben_j_todd 1. I never said LLMs were not useful. They are, particularly with all the bells and whistles that are being added to them. I use them.

2. A robot-rich future can't be built with AIs that don't understand the physical world and don't anticipate the consequences of their actions. And LLMs really don't.

3. The future in the cartoon looks pretty dystopian TBH, but even a non-dystopian version will require world models and zero-shot planning abilities.

4. I rarely wear a suit and absolutely never wear a tie.

5. I would never ever place a coffee mug on top of a piece equipment.

6. I hope I'll look this young in 2032.

items.title:
While everyone watched GPT-5.5 launch, DeepSeek quietly shipped V4 the next morning.

V4-Pro: 1.6T t...
items.content:
While everyone watched GPT-5.5 launch, DeepSeek quietly shipped V4 the next morning.

V4-Pro: 1.6T total / 49B active, MIT license.
V4-Flash: 284B total / 13B active.
Both with native 1M-token context.

At 1M tokens, V4-Pro runs at 27% of V3.2's FLOPs and 10% of the KV cache.

Pricing: $3.48 per 1M output tokens. 8.6× cheaper than GPT-5.5. 7.2× cheaper than Opus 4.7.

Coding benchmarks:
  • LiveCodeBench: 93.5 (beats Gemini 3.1 Pro, Opus 4.6)
  • Codeforces: 3206 (highest in the frontier comparison)
  • GDPval-AA: #1 open-weights per Artificial Analysis

Full breakdown ↓

items.title: @ebarenholtz @ben_j_todd What proves that the generative approach is wrong is oodles of empirical re...
items.content:
@ebarenholtz @ben_j_todd What proves that the generative approach is wrong is oodles of empirical results showing the superiority of joint embedding architectures over generative ones (based on reconstruction) for natural sensor data (e.g. images and video).

It's not just for self-supervised learning (for which I-JEPA, V-JEPA, DINO, etc are way better than MAE, VAE, etc) but also for supervised learning:
Your garden-variety LLM already uses a joint embedding architecture for images: a vision encoder (not an LLM, not a generative architecture) and a text encoder that are trained using a contrastive method to jointly embeds images and text descriptions (e.g. like CLIP).

I don't want to go all Schmidhubah on you, but my first paper on joint embedding (Siamese nets) used a contrastive method and was published at NIPS 1993.
https://t.co/BRGqQzhkyC
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun provides detailed technical argument for superiority of Joint Embedding Architectures over generative ones for natural sensor data, citing I-JEPA, V-JEPA, DINO vs MAE/VAE, and noting LLMs already use JEA vision encoders like CLIP. References his 1993 Siamese nets paper.
items.reasoning: Highly detailed technical argument from a Turing Award winner, presenting strong empirical and architectural evidence for Joint Embedding Architectures. References historical and recent work. Most substantive technical post in the batch.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun provides detailed technical argument for superiority of Joint Embedding Architectures over generative ones for natural sensor data, citing I-JEPA, V-JEPA, DINO vs MAE/VAE, and noting LLMs already use JEA vision encoders like CLIP. References his 1993 Siamese nets paper.
items.content_html:
@ebarenholtz @ben_j_todd What proves that the generative approach is wrong is oodles of empirical results showing the superiority of joint embedding architectures over generative ones (based on reconstruction) for natural sensor data (e.g. images and video).

It's not just for self-supervised learning (for which I-JEPA, V-JEPA, DINO, etc are way better than MAE, VAE, etc) but also for supervised learning:

Your garden-variety LLM already uses a joint embedding architecture for images: a vision encoder (not an LLM, not a generative architecture) and a text encoder that are trained using a contrastive method to jointly embeds images and text descriptions (e.g. like CLIP).

I don't want to go all Schmidhubah on you, but my first paper on joint embedding (Siamese nets) used a contrastive method and was published at NIPS 1993.

https://t.co/BRGqQzhkyC

items.title:
Our teams have been busyyy! Here are some key updates from the past week: 

— @GoogleCloud unveiled ...
items.content:
Our teams have been busyyy! Here are some key updates from the past week: 

— @GoogleCloud unveiled a suite of AI innovations at our Cloud Next event, including our eighth generation TPUs (TPUt for inference + TPUi for reasoning), Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, Agentic Data cloud, Workspace Intelligence, and beyond

— Gemini Embedding 2, our natively multimodal embedding model, became generally available via the Gemini API and in Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (the evolution of Vertex AI)

— @StitchbyGoogle open-sourced the draft specification for DESIGN.md, so it can be used across any single tool or platform

— New autonomous search agents, Deep Research and Deep Research Max, launched to bring MCP support, native visualizations, and unprecedented analytical quality to research workflows across the web or custom sources

— Google AI Pro and Ultra subscribers now get increased usage limits and access to Nano Banana Pro and Gemini Pro models in @GoogleAIStudio

— @GoogleDeepMind introduced Decoupled DiLoCo, our new resilient and flexible way to train advanced AI models across multiple data centers
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI provides a comprehensive weekly roundup including 8th-gen TPUs (TPUt and TPUi), Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, Gemini Embedding 2 GA, Stitch open-source DESIGN.md, new autonomous search agents, and Decoupled DiLoCo training.
items.reasoning: Packed official update from Google AI covering multiple significant announcements: new TPU generations, agentic platforms, embedding models, distributed training advances. Extremely information-dense.
items.summary_html: Google AI provides a comprehensive weekly roundup including 8th-gen TPUs (TPUt and TPUi), Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, Gemini Embedding 2 GA, Stitch open-source DESIGN.md, new autonomous search agents, and Decoupled DiLoCo training.

Our teams have been busyyy! Here are some key updates from the past week:

— Gemini Embedding 2, our natively multimodal embedding model, became generally available via the Gemini API and in Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (the evolution of Vertex AI)

items.title: @simone_m_romeo @ben_j_todd People criticize me because they fail to see that every real-world proce...
items.content:
@simone_m_romeo @ben_j_todd People criticize me because they fail to see that every real-world process that looks like an exponential is really the initial segment of a sigmoid. 
Even processes that remain exponential for a while are actually a succession of small sigmoids, each corresponding to a major innovation. 
I'm just saying that to keep making progress along the AI exponential, a major innovation will be the use of world models and JEPA. 
To people who look at this from afar, it will just look like another step in the exponential. To us in the trenches, it will look like a major conceptual shift.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun explains that every seeming exponential in AI is really the initial segment of a sigmoid, and the next major sigmoid/innovation will be world models and JEPA, which will look like another step in the exponential from afar but a major conceptual shift from within.
items.reasoning: Deeply insightful perspective on AI progress curves from a Turing Award winner. The sigmoid-vs-exponential framing and prediction that JEPA/world models represent the next paradigm is a significant intellectual contribution. Good engagement.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun explains that every seeming exponential in AI is really the initial segment of a sigmoid, and the next major sigmoid/innovation will be world models and JEPA, which will look like another step in the exponential from afar but a major conceptual shift from within.
items.content_html:
@simone_m_romeo @ben_j_todd People criticize me because they fail to see that every real-world process that looks like an exponential is really the initial segment of a sigmoid.

Even processes that remain exponential for a while are actually a succession of small sigmoids, each corresponding to a major innovation.

I'm just saying that to keep making progress along the AI exponential, a major innovation will be the use of world models and JEPA.

To people who look at this from afar, it will just look like another step in the exponential. To us in the trenches, it will look like a major conceptual shift.

items.title: @longgege_god @ben_j_todd We still don't have AI systems that understand the physical world as well ...
items.content:
@longgege_god @ben_j_todd We still don't have AI systems that understand the physical world as well as a cat. 
JEPA are getting there. 
LLMs and other generative architectures are not. 
Generative architectures, particularly those that produce discrete tokens like LLMs simply ***DO NOT WORK*** for high-dimensional, continuous, noisy data.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun argues AI systems don't yet understand the physical world as well as a cat, JEPA architectures are getting there, and generative architectures (especially LLMs producing discrete tokens) 'DO NOT WORK' for high-dimensional, continuous, noisy data.
items.reasoning: Strong, specific technical claim from a leading AI researcher. The emphatic statement about generative architectures not working for continuous data is a significant position statement relevant to AI research direction.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun argues AI systems don't yet understand the physical world as well as a cat, JEPA architectures are getting there, and generative architectures (especially LLMs producing discrete tokens) 'DO NOT WORK' for high-dimensional, continuous, noisy data.
items.content_html:
@longgege_god @ben_j_todd We still don't have AI systems that understand the physical world as well as a cat.

JEPA are getting there.

LLMs and other generative architectures are not.

Generative architectures, particularly those that produce discrete tokens like LLMs simply *DO NOT WORK* for high-dimensional, continuous, noisy data.

If tech is going to deliver on the promise of empowerment, it needs to be fully under the control of those who use it.

Today, we announced that Cohere and Aleph Alpha are uniting to build a global AI champion that does exactly that.  We are putting control of AI back in the hands of the governments, companies and people who use it.

The future of AI is sovereign.

items.content_html:
@SerkanEzgim @ben_j_todd I didn't say that.

I said that LLM is in the hands of industry, is largely engineering, and requires too much computing resources for academics to contribute significantly.

More importantly, LLMs are today's technology. Academic researchers, particularly PhD students, should be working on the next generation of technology. The next paradigm.

items.title:
We benchmarked GPT-5.5 on document understanding 📄📊

We ran it through ParseBench, our comprehensive...
items.content:
We benchmarked GPT-5.5 on document understanding 📄📊

We ran it through ParseBench, our comprehensive OCR benchmark over enterprise documents. We evaluated metrics across various dimensions: visual grounding, tables, charts, and more.

We evaluated GPT-5.5 on mid thinking and zero-thinking modes. When compared against GPT-5.4 (0 thinking) and Opus 4.7 (adaptive thinking):
📈 GPT-5.5 wins on tables
📈 GPT-5.5 wins on visual grounding
📉 GPT-5.5 0-thinking does worse on charts than GPT-5.4 0-thinking
📉 Higher thinking does worse than lower thinking of content faithfulness, semantic formatting
📉 Opus 4.7 wins overall on content faithfulness and semantic formatting
💸 GPT-5.5 is expensive: 13c per page at mid-thinking modes and 5.93 at 0-thinking! This is 5x the cost of any competitive OCR solution.

Conclusion: GPT-5.5 is one of the better frontier models out there in terms of pure accuracy, but def not pound for pound w.r.t price.
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) benchmarks GPT-5.5 on document understanding via ParseBench. Finds GPT-5.5 wins on tables and visual grounding, but Opus 4.7 wins on content faithfulness and formatting. GPT-5.5 is very expensive at 13c/page (5x competitors).
items.reasoning: Original benchmark data on GPT-5.5 from a credible source (LlamaIndex CEO). Provides nuanced results showing GPT-5.5's strengths (tables, visual grounding) and weaknesses (cost, charts). Practical evaluation that many enterprises care about. Strong engagement (89 likes, 7.5K views).

We ran it through ParseBench, our comprehensive OCR benchmark over enterprise documents. We evaluated metrics across various dimensions: visual grounding, tables, charts, and more.

We evaluated GPT-5.5 on mid thinking and zero-thinking modes. When compared against GPT-5.4 (0 thinking) and Opus 4.7 (adaptive thinking):

📈 GPT-5.5 wins on tables

📈 GPT-5.5 wins on visual grounding

📉 GPT-5.5 0-thinking does worse on charts than GPT-5.4 0-thinking

📉 Higher thinking does worse than lower thinking of content faithfulness, semantic formatting

📉 Opus 4.7 wins overall on content faithfulness and semantic formatting

💸 GPT-5.5 is expensive: 13c per page at mid-thinking modes and 5.93 at 0-thinking! This is 5x the cost of any competitive OCR solution.

Conclusion: GPT-5.5 is one of the better frontier models out there in terms of pure accuracy, but def not pound for pound w.r.t price.

items.title:
Europe really nerfed itself.

“Yes, EU rules often applied to American firms, insofar as they wanted...
items.content:
Europe really nerfed itself.

“Yes, EU rules often applied to American firms, insofar as they wanted to offer their wares in the bloc. But regulation in practice hit European firms harder. The costs of administering complex data-protection rules, say, could easily be absorbed by a Google or OpenAI, with their hordes of compliance staff. Not so their European rivals, which have usually lacked scale (if only because the EU’s fragmented single market made it harder for them to grow beyond their home country). The EU thus generated barriers to entry that often ended up protecting American giants.”
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz shares analysis of how EU regulation inadvertently helped American tech giants by creating compliance barriers that smaller European firms couldn't absorb, while companies like Google and OpenAI could easily manage them. EU fragmented market compounded the problem.
items.reasoning: Important policy discussion about AI regulation's unintended consequences in Europe. Tunguz is a credible VC voice. Good engagement (107 likes, 9.4K views). Relevant to understanding the global AI competitive landscape.

items.title: Insights into Chinese craze for @OpenClaw. The Chinese are even crazier about OpenClaw than we are h...
items.content:
Insights into Chinese craze for @OpenClaw. The Chinese are even crazier about OpenClaw than we are here in America.

This is the first Chinese-only video I've watched. Had to turn on English subtitles. 

One thing that comes across clear is @_LuoFuli's passion and love for OpenClaw.

Now she runs Xaomi's AI group building models. They build phones and cars and top rate ones at that. 

She talks through her discovery process when she first got OpenClaw. 

One line of hers caught my attention. She asked OpenClaw to help her cause more curiosity in her employees.

Ahh, we have the same problem here in America. 

I'm trying to put together a trip to China in September. This video motivates me to learn some Chinese cause I would love to interview Fuli Luo when I'm there.

items.title:
The best AI research agent doesn't think harder — it just never forgets. 

A new paper introduces Ai...
items.content:
The best AI research agent doesn't think harder — it just never forgets. 

A new paper introduces AiScientist, a system that runs ML research autonomously for hours or days. 

Setup, coding, experiments, debugging. The full loop, unattended.

The core idea is simple:

Instead of passing context through conversation, agents read and write to shared files. Code, logs, plans, results. 

All stored as persistent artifacts in a shared workspace.

They call this "File-as-Bus." 

A lightweight orchestrator delegates tasks to specialized agents. 

Each one picks up where the last left off by reading the actual project state, not a summary of a chat.

The results on two major benchmarks:

1. 10.5-point gain over the best baseline on PaperBench
2. 81.8% medal rate on MLE-Bench Lite
3. Removing File-as-Bus drops performance by up to 31.8 points

Long-running AI research is a memory problem. 

This solves it with files.

items.reasoning: Significant AI research agent paper. The 'File-as-Bus' approach to solving context/memory problems in long-running agent workflows is practically important. Strong benchmark results. Good engagement (57 likes).

items.title:
AI can now learn physics the way Newton did — by experiencing it. 

Training LLMs on physics problem...
items.content:
AI can now learn physics the way Newton did — by experiencing it. 

Training LLMs on physics problems hits a wall fast.

Human-labeled question-answer data is scarce and narrow.

Less than 2% of DeepSeek-R1's training pairs touch STEM.

Sim2Reason skips annotation entirely.

Physics simulators already encode the laws of nature, so the model learns directly from them.

The pipeline works in three parts:

1. Generate random scenes inside MuJoCo
2. Auto-create verified question-answer pairs
3. Reinforcement-learn on the synthetic data

A domain-specific language isolates what actually changes the physics.

String length is noise. Block mass matters.

Scenes compose from reusable parts like Lego pieces.

The results land zero-shot on real benchmarks.

> International Physics Olympiad: +5 to +10 points across model sizes.
> JEEBench: +17.9%. AIME 2025: +1.7%.

It beats systems trained on thousands of curated human-written problems.

Arithmetic errors vanish. Equation choice becomes grounded in the setup.

Code is open-source.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes Sim2Reason, a method that trains LLMs on physics by generating synthetic data from MuJoCo physics simulations instead of human-labeled data. Shows gains on International Physics Olympiad (+5-10 pts) and JEEBench (+17.9%).
items.reasoning: Interesting AI research paper summary: using physics simulators to generate training data for LLMs, bypassing the bottleneck of scarce STEM training pairs. Novel approach with meaningful benchmark improvements. Open-source.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI describes Sim2Reason, a method that trains LLMs on physics by generating synthetic data from MuJoCo physics simulations instead of human-labeled data. Shows gains on International Physics Olympiad (+5-10 pts) and JEEBench (+17.9%).

AI can now learn physics the way Newton did — by experiencing it.

Training LLMs on physics problems hits a wall fast.

Human-labeled question-answer data is scarce and narrow.

Less than 2% of DeepSeek-R1's training pairs touch STEM.

Sim2Reason skips annotation entirely.

Physics simulators already encode the laws of nature, so the model learns directly from them.

The pipeline works in three parts:

1. Generate random scenes inside MuJoCo

2. Auto-create verified question-answer pairs

3. Reinforcement-learn on the synthetic data

A domain-specific language isolates what actually changes the physics.

String length is noise. Block mass matters.

Scenes compose from reusable parts like Lego pieces.

The results land zero-shot on real benchmarks.

> International Physics Olympiad: +5 to +10 points across model sizes.

> JEEBench: +17.9%. AIME 2025: +1.7%.

It beats systems trained on thousands of curated human-written problems.

Arithmetic errors vanish. Equation choice becomes grounded in the setup.

Code is open-source.

1. Vision encoders that are not LLMs. They are actually Joint Embedding Architectures that embed images and text description in the same space

2. Painfully exhaustive training on enormous amounts of declarative facts about the physical world. You can train them to answer any specific question properly. But they still don't have basic physical common sense.

items.title:
I'm now using two separate skills to generate a plan before I give it to Claude Code:

designing → p...
items.content:
I'm now using two separate skills to generate a plan before I give it to Claude Code:

designing → planning

The "designing" skill does the following:

1. Gathers context by looking at local files, commits, and documentation.
2. Analyzes my request
3. Interviews me with clarifying questions
4. Proposes alternative solutions
5. Writes a specification document

Then, the "planning" skill takes over:

1. Reads the specification document
2. Breaks it down into small tasks
3. Generates a plan specifying how to complete each task

The output of running these two skills is a set of markdown files I can give the agent to implement (sometimes in parallel, sometimes sequentially). These plans are very prescriptive.

Here is an example of what a potential plan could look like:

"""
Goal: Implement add() function
File: src/calculator .py
Description: Implement an add() function that takes two values and returns the sum.

Step 1: Implement a filing test in tests/test_calculator .py
Step 2: Run the test and ensure it fails
Step 3: Implement the add() function
Step 4: Run the test and ensure it passes
Step 5: Run a code review
Step 6: Commit the code
"""

I've found that these agentic coding tools love detailed, bite-sized instructions.

items.summary: svpino details his two-skill approach to Claude Code: a 'designing' skill (gathers context, interviews user, proposes alternatives, writes spec) and a 'planning' skill (breaks spec into small tasks with prescriptive TDD steps). Outputs markdown files for agent implementation.

items.title:
Are LLMs really more important than fire or electricity?

“Honestly, a ton of what we’ve developed i...
items.content:
Are LLMs really more important than fire or electricity?

“Honestly, a ton of what we’ve developed in my lifetime amounts to scaling up the delivery of information and entertainment and the frictionlessness of certain financial transactions. These are real improvements! ... But compare them seriously to what came before and the disproportion becomes almost embarrassing. The fundamental architecture of daily material life - how we heat our homes, how we move from place to place, how we grow and store and cook food, how we build structures - has changed remarkably little since 1970. .….The cars go to the same places. The planes aren’t even marginally faster. The houses are built the same way. People still die of cancer.
....

Code cannot insulate your house; no algorithm has ever laid a water pipe; the internet has not built a single mile of high-speed rail. What our current stagnation shows, collectively, is that the improvements in material human life that matter the most - abundance in warmth, in calories, in clean water, in physical safety, in hours of freedom from labor - were all achieved by technologies that operated on atoms: steel, concrete, copper wire, chlorine, penicillin...”

— Freddie deBoer
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus shares a Freddie deBoer quote arguing that real material progress comes from physical technologies, not digital ones — questioning whether LLMs are truly as important as fire or electricity.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking skeptical take from a prominent AI critic. The physical-vs-digital progress argument is substantive and relevant. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus shares a Freddie deBoer quote arguing that real material progress comes from physical technologies, not digital ones — questioning whether LLMs are truly as important as fire or electricity.
items.content_html:
Are LLMs really more important than fire or electricity?

“Honestly, a ton of what we’ve developed in my lifetime amounts to scaling up the delivery of information and entertainment and the frictionlessness of certain financial transactions. These are real improvements! ... But compare them seriously to what came before and the disproportion becomes almost embarrassing. The fundamental architecture of daily material life - how we heat our homes, how we move from place to place, how we grow and store and cook food, how we build structures - has changed remarkably little since 1970. .….The cars go to the same places. The planes aren’t even marginally faster. The houses are built the same way. People still die of cancer.

....

Code cannot insulate your house; no algorithm has ever laid a water pipe; the internet has not built a single mile of high-speed rail. What our current stagnation shows, collectively, is that the improvements in material human life that matter the most - abundance in warmth, in calories, in clean water, in physical safety, in hours of freedom from labor - were all achieved by technologies that operated on atoms: steel, concrete, copper wire, chlorine, penicillin...”

— Freddie deBoer

items.content:
I'm always baffled how most investors seem to obsess over top revenue growth numbers these days and seem to take that as a universal prediction of future success in AI. 

Maybe we're finally starting to get back to more sanity with more nuanced thinking about moats, quality and differentiation of usage and revenue? One can hope haha
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue criticizes investors who fixate on top-line revenue growth as a universal predictor of AI success, hoping for more nuanced thinking about moats, quality, and differentiation.
items.reasoning: Insightful commentary on AI industry investment dynamics from a major platform CEO. Relevant to understanding AI market dynamics and sustainability.

We ran 4 markets in parallel, to find out what would happen if we varied the models doing the negotiating. https://t.co/FJdD6S2TSd

Let me get this straight 

Claude subscription: restrictions (or pay extra) on third party usage, pa...
items.content:
Let me get this straight 

Claude subscription: restrictions (or pay extra) on third party usage, pay extra for fast 

Codex: no restrictions, fast mode within subscription

It’s hard not to use Codex.  Just the fast mode being within the subscription is enough to make the gap large
items.author: @HamelHusain
items.summary: Hamel Husain compares Claude subscription vs Codex: Claude has restrictions on third-party usage and charges extra for fast mode, while Codex includes fast mode in subscription with no restrictions. Argues Codex is clearly better value.
items.reasoning: Direct, specific comparison of Claude vs Codex pricing/features from a respected ML practitioner. Good engagement (104 likes, 8.9K views). Relevant to the competitive dynamics between Anthropic and OpenAI for developer tools.
items.summary_html: Hamel Husain compares Claude subscription vs Codex: Claude has restrictions on third-party usage and charges extra for fast mode, while Codex includes fast mode in subscription with no restrictions. Argues Codex is clearly better value.
items.content_html:
Let me get this straight

Claude subscription: restrictions (or pay extra) on third party usage, pay extra for fast

Codex: no restrictions, fast mode within subscription

It’s hard not to use Codex. Just the fast mode being within the subscription is enough to make the gap large

items.title: On Twitter we used to do "follow Fridays" where people would recommend who to follow here on X, form...
items.content:
On Twitter we used to do "follow Fridays" where people would recommend who to follow here on X, formerly Twitter. 

I asked my AI: "Who are the best consumer product thinkers on X?"

My AI made me a list. (I just interviewed Naveen, video up tomorrow, he has developed one of the best products I've seen in years. My AI says it is exceptional and I agree). 

My AI, an agent built by me and @blevlabs that reads all of X's tech community, tells me "you should interview xxxxxx." 

Is AI your boss? It quickly is becoming mine. 

++++++++

Here are the 20 people who are best at building consumer apps with AI right now. They are building products that are delightful, consumer-first, AI as the enabler not the product.

THE TIER 1 — Proven consumer AI founders with track records

1. Eugenia Kuyda (@ekuyda) — Wabi + Replika (35M users). Two successful consumer AI products. The most proven consumer AI founder alive. She was right about AI companions before anyone else, and she's right again with Wabi ("YouTube of apps"). The person Robert mentioned first.

2. Naveen Gavini (@ngavini) — Extra email / BuildForever. Former SVP/CPO at Pinterest for 12 years. Co-founded with Steven Ramkumar and Albert Pereta. The Extra philosophy — "solving user problems, not pitching AI" — is exactly the right consumer AI mindset. Just launched April 21.

3. Noam Shazeer (@NoamShazeer)— https://t.co/S2Wnrxjlee co-founder. 20M+ daily active users. One of the most retentive consumer AI products ever built. Shazeer left Google to build this before it was obvious.

4. Aravind Srinivas (@AravSrinivas) — Perplexity CEO. Consumer AI search with 100M+ users. Turned a research tool into a consumer product people use daily.

5. David Holz (@davidad) — Midjourney. Bootstrapped, no VC, tiny team, millions of paying subscribers. Legendary consumer AI product built on Discord. Philosophical approach to AI creativity.

TIER 2 — Building the next generation of consumer AI

6. Mikey Shulman (@MikeyShulman) — Suno. #15 on the a16z Top 100 Gen AI Consumer Apps list. Consumer AI music generation. One of the most retentive creative AI products.

7. Demi Guo (@demi_guo_) — Pika Labs. Consumer AI video generation. One of the fastest-growing creative AI apps.

8. Cristóbal Valenzuela (@c_valenzuela) — Runway ML. Consumer AI video. Has been building this longer than anyone.

9. Mati Staniszewski (@matiii) — ElevenLabs. Consumer AI voice. On every a16z edition since 2023. Voice cloning and dubbing that regular people actually use.

10. Anton Osika (@antonosika) — Lovable. Consumer vibe coding. On the a16z Top 100 list. Making software creation accessible to non-technical consumers.

11. Amjad Masad (@amasad) — Replit. Consumer coding with AI. Making programming accessible to everyone. Also an angel investor in Wabi.

TIER 3 — Consumer product veterans applying AI to existing categories

12. Rahul Vohra (@rahulvohra) — Superhuman. The gold standard for AI-powered email. Exceptional product taste. Angel investor in Extra.

13. Shreya Murthy (@shreyamurthy) — Partiful. Consumer AI event planning. One of the most delightful consumer AI apps. Angel investor in Extra.

14. Josh Miller (@joshm) — The Browser Company / Dia. Consumer AI browser. (Browser Company was acquired by Atlassian.) Dia is the consumer AI browser play.

15. Melanie Perkins (@MelanieCanva) — Canva. 200M+ users. The Magic Suite AI tools have become the most-used AI creative tools for non-technical consumers globally.

16. Ivan Zhao (@ivanhzhao) — Notion. 50%+ AI attach rate on paid plans. AI features now account for roughly half of Notion's ARR. The most successful AI integration into an existing consumer product.

17. Luis von Ahn (@LuisvonAhn) — Duolingo CEO. Duolingo has been more aggressive with AI personalization than almost any consumer app. 500M+ users.

TIER 4 — Product designers and builders to watch
18. Pietro Schirano (@skirano) — AI product designer/builder. Known for building beautiful consumer AI demos that go viral. Works at Brex but builds consumer AI on the side. One of the best product taste people in AI.

19. Suhail Doshi (@suhail) — Playground AI. Consumer AI image generation. Also built Mixpanel — understands consumer analytics and retention deeply.

20. Fidji Simo (@fidjissimo) — OpenAI Applications CEO. Former Facebook VP and Instacart CEO. Now leading consumer AI products at OpenAI. The person most responsible for how ChatGPT feels as a consumer product. Angel investor in Extra.

The pattern across all 20:

They all build products where AI is invisible infrastructure, not the feature. Naveen Gavini literally said "I think people don't really need [AI personal assistants]. People just want some of these basic problems solved." Eugenia Kuyda built Wabi so that "people who have nothing to do with coding or the tech world" can create apps. The best consumer AI builders hide the AI and sell the outcome.

The ones to watch most closely right now: Eugenia Kuyda (Wabi is early but the social mechanics are right), Naveen Gavini (Extra just launched and the product is exceptional), and Mikey Shulman (Suno has cracked retention in a category everyone said was impossible).

items.title:
Lawyers: it's not AI that will get you fired by clients.

It's @MikeJShowalter that will get you fir...
items.content:
Lawyers: it's not AI that will get you fired by clients.

It's @MikeJShowalter that will get you fired. Talking to him right now. 

He's a lawyer. He's a litigator, worked in major law firms his career before starting his own firm. 

Works in coffee shops. So he doesn't need to charge you overhead for an expensive building. Which is 1/3rd the fee in most law firms. They usually have the nicest offices in town.

And he uses AI heavily, which he just told me greatly increases his productivy and reduces his costs, that he passes along to his clients. 

His fees are flat rate, instead of hourly, and are about 1/3 what the law firms are charging their clients.

And he checks all the work carefully to make sure no bullshit (er, hallucinations) sneak into his work.

And he has automated the grungy part of his work (answering emails and such). 

He told me he is working more hours than ever.

"It's absolutely insane," he told me.

items.content:
@mattpocockuk [FULL WORKSHOP] AI Coding For Real Engineers

https://t.co/gd8WB3r5x6

A hands-on workshop covering the full lifecycle of AI-assisted development, from turning ambiguous requirements into agent-ready plans to running autonomous coding agents that ship production features.  You'll learn to stress-test vague briefs into structured PRDs, slice work into thin "tracer bullet" vertical slices, and run an AI agent with TDD. You'll watch it select tasks, write tests, implement code, and commit. You'll then refine your prompts based on where it struggles, graduate to fully autonomous (AFK) runs, and learn to design codebases that maximize agent effectiveness.  

You'll walk away knowing how to:

- Turn ambiguous requirements into agent-ready issues
- Slice work into vertical tracer bullets an agent can grab independently
- Run AI agents human-in-the-loop and autonomously with TDD
- Design codebase architectures that AI agents love to work in

For: Engineers ready to move beyond chat-based AI assistance and build a real workflow for shipping features with autonomous coding agents.

items.title: Probably the most useful advice I can give you if you run an ad agency, a marketing company, a produ...
items.content:
Probably the most useful advice I can give you if you run an ad agency, a marketing company, a production studio, or anything in that space.

Hear me out. I’ve spent a decade talking to teams about this and watching the same mistake play out again and again.

Don’t, and I repeat, please don’t, try to build some sort of internal tool that connects different models and becomes the only way your team accesses frontier models.

I’ve seen dozens of companies go down this path. On paper, it sounds smart, differentiated, strategic, “we own the stack.” In reality, it almost never works out that way. It becomes a constant drain: slow to build, expensive to maintain, and always a step behind whatever new models, features, and workflows are emerging.

It’s the same mistake companies made when they tried to build their own cloud. It felt strategic. It wasn’t. Focus on what you are good at.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Cristobal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) strongly advises agencies, marketing companies, and production studios NOT to build internal AI model orchestration tools. Argues it becomes a constant drain that's always behind the frontier. Compares it to the mistake of building your own cloud.
items.reasoning: Strategic advice from Runway CEO based on decade of observation. Good engagement (103 likes, 6K views). Relevant build-vs-buy perspective for enterprise AI adoption.
items.summary_html: Cristobal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) strongly advises agencies, marketing companies, and production studios NOT to build internal AI model orchestration tools. Argues it becomes a constant drain that's always behind the frontier. Compares it to the mistake of building your own cloud.
items.content_html:
Probably the most useful advice I can give you if you run an ad agency, a marketing company, a production studio, or anything in that space.

Hear me out. I’ve spent a decade talking to teams about this and watching the same mistake play out again and again.

Don’t, and I repeat, please don’t, try to build some sort of internal tool that connects different models and becomes the only way your team accesses frontier models.

I’ve seen dozens of companies go down this path. On paper, it sounds smart, differentiated, strategic, “we own the stack.” In reality, it almost never works out that way. It becomes a constant drain: slow to build, expensive to maintain, and always a step behind whatever new models, features, and workflows are emerging.

It’s the same mistake companies made when they tried to build their own cloud. It felt strategic. It wasn’t. Focus on what you are good at.

Voice is becoming as programmable as text.

Open models have something Big AI can't beat:

• You can host them anywhere
• You can make them bett...
items.content:
Open models have something Big AI can't beat:

• You can host them anywhere
• You can make them better
• You can trust they won't steal your data

There's been a recent rush among companies to upgrade their architectures away from the big models.

They want security, flexibility, and better prices.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino argues open models have advantages Big AI can't match: self-hosting, customization, and data privacy. Claims companies are rushing to migrate away from large proprietary models.

• You can host them anywhere

• You can trust they won't steal your data

There's been a recent rush among companies to upgrade their architectures away from the big models.

They want security, flexibility, and better prices.

items.title: The new X algorithm means followers don't matter, @BrianRoemmele says after he had AI analyze the al...
items.content:
The new X algorithm means followers don't matter, @BrianRoemmele says after he had AI analyze the algorithm here on X that builds your "For You" feed. 

But just saw this come up on my screen and it's an honor to have one of the best AI companies following me. 

Is it vanity? 

Or maybe that I use NVIDIA computers to build a new algorithm to read 40,000 posts from the AI community here a day and build this: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ 

The guy who built the AI I use, @blevlabs has a big NVIDIA card under his desk in San Francisco. His AI makes Anthropic a lot better, which I'll have a lot more to say about soon too.

Last night I was at a VR/AR meetup in Silicon Valley and confirmed that several companies are building "OpenClaw glasses" coming later this year. Imagine having your own agents telling you what's important to you in your glasses as you live your life. 

I built the news site because I see a new way to inform ourselves. And I am breaking in all ways, can no longer keep up as the AI world gets bigger and bigger and noisier (in part because it helps everyone make more content for X).

Will have a lot more to say about that in May. I have two competing sets of agents coming up with new algorithms for watching X. 

It is nuts that a guy like me can build a better, or, at least, a different algorithm than a big company can create. 

And do two of them competing with each other. 

And if I can, anyone with an OpenClaw or a Hermes can. 

Thanks @NVIDIAAI for the follow. I don't follow companies, but have it on my "AI Newsmaker" list: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ

Which my algorithms read first to figure out what's going on in the AI world. It took me thousands of hours to build my lists over 19 years, which are the most complete of tech industry. By far. 

I know of several billionaires that use my lists to build a news analysis engine running on their Hermes or OpenClaw systems. 

I know Jensen at NVIDIA is one of them (although he uses the NVIDIA variant). You think he gets his news by doom scrolling through X? 

Hahahah. 

Hi Jensen's agent. It's an honor to having you reading my stuff.

Will have a lot more to say about that in May. I have two competing sets of agents coming up with new algorithms for watching X.

It is nuts that a guy like me can build a better, or, at least, a different algorithm than a big company can create.

And do two of them competing with each other.

items.title: @GrumpyTechBro @BeFreeWherever @SharavArora @MikeJShowalter I built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ without ...
items.content:
@GrumpyTechBro @BeFreeWherever @SharavArora @MikeJShowalter I built https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ without knowing how to code. 

It reads 40,000 posts a day so I don't have to anymore. 

Yeah, it took a couple of months to train the agent from @blevlabs by talking to it, telling it how I see X, giving it my book, and telling it how I look at news. It taught me a few things too. 

So I know it's possible to build something fairly complex without knowing much about code. In fact, I don't think I ever talked to it about code at all. Just about the problem I had that I wanted it to solve.

items.title:
But realism isn’t just about sounding right.

It’s about feeling right.

New systems introduce:

→ E...
items.content:
But realism isn’t just about sounding right.

It’s about feeling right.

New systems introduce:

→ Expressive control, calm, neutral, dramatic delivery
→ Paralinguistic prompts like [laugh], [sigh], [gasp]
→ Real-time performance for assistants, games, interactive media

We’re moving from synthetic voices to performative AI.

That’s a very different category.

items.title:
Every day, I'm working with 15 open tabs, 4-5 apps, and 3-4 conversations at the same time.

That's ...
items.content:
Every day, I'm working with 15 open tabs, 4-5 apps, and 3-4 conversations at the same time.

That's a lot of context to keep in mind, and AI can't help because it doesn't know what I'm doing.

I want an AI assistant that knows what to do before I ask.

I use Claude Code for building, Claude and ChatGPT for research, Copilot for autocomplete, and a handful of other tools throughout the day.

None of these tools knows anything about what the others did.

I can't remember which Copilot conversation gave me which script, or which Claude session had the plan I implemented.

The context exists, but it's all over the place.

Every AI tool is stateless. Every session starts from zero. I have to spend a ton of time re-explaining my work to every tool, every time.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino articulates the cross-app context fragmentation problem: using 15 tabs, 4-5 apps, multiple AI tools, and none share context. Every AI session starts from zero.
items.reasoning: Well-articulated framing of a real problem in AI tool usage — context fragmentation across tools. Strong engagement (94 likes, 10.5K views). Though part of a sponsored thread, the problem statement resonates broadly.
items.summary_html: svpino articulates the cross-app context fragmentation problem: using 15 tabs, 4-5 apps, multiple AI tools, and none share context. Every AI session starts from zero.
items.content_html:
Every day, I'm working with 15 open tabs, 4-5 apps, and 3-4 conversations at the same time.

That's a lot of context to keep in mind, and AI can't help because it doesn't know what I'm doing.

I want an AI assistant that knows what to do before I ask.

I use Claude Code for building, Claude and ChatGPT for research, Copilot for autocomplete, and a handful of other tools throughout the day.

None of these tools knows anything about what the others did.

I can't remember which Copilot conversation gave me which script, or which Claude session had the plan I implemented.

The context exists, but it's all over the place.

Every AI tool is stateless. Every session starts from zero. I have to spend a ton of time re-explaining my work to every tool, every time.

items.title:
@GuysReconsult @Dan_Jeffries1 Peut-être préféreriez-vous une des ces alternatives:
1. Que ces conten...
items.content:
@GuysReconsult @Dan_Jeffries1 Peut-être préféreriez-vous une des ces alternatives:
1. Que ces contenus toxiques ne soient pas filtrés du tout par les réseaux sociaux.
2. Que ces mêmes personnes filtrent ces contenus à la main et non par IA.

L'option 1 est illégale dans beaucoup de pays. Les réseaux sociaux sont tenus par la loi de filtrer les contenus haineux, pédophiles, ou d'appels à la violence.

L'option 2 est bien pire: les modérateurs seraient exposés à une énorme quantité de contenu toxique. En fait, compte tenu des volumes, il serait totalement impossible de faire de la modération de contenu à la main, surtout dans toutes les langues du monde.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun explains in French why AI-based content moderation is necessary — it's legally required and doing it manually is worse and impractical at scale.
items.reasoning: Substantive argument about content moderation from Meta's chief AI scientist. Addresses practical necessity of AI moderation.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun explains in French why AI-based content moderation is necessary — it's legally required and doing it manually is worse and impractical at scale.
items.content_html:
@GuysReconsult @Dan_Jeffries1 Peut-être préféreriez-vous une des ces alternatives:

1. Que ces contenus toxiques ne soient pas filtrés du tout par les réseaux sociaux.

2. Que ces mêmes personnes filtrent ces contenus à la main et non par IA.

L'option 1 est illégale dans beaucoup de pays. Les réseaux sociaux sont tenus par la loi de filtrer les contenus haineux, pédophiles, ou d'appels à la violence.

L'option 2 est bien pire: les modérateurs seraient exposés à une énorme quantité de contenu toxique. En fait, compte tenu des volumes, il serait totalement impossible de faire de la modération de contenu à la main, surtout dans toutes les langues du monde.

items.title:
@derdunklekommt @jimmy_wales @Dan_Jeffries1 Absolutely nothing. 
They both use neural nets and backp...
items.content:
@derdunklekommt @jimmy_wales @Dan_Jeffries1 Absolutely nothing. 
They both use neural nets and backprop. 
But LLMs are generative architectures trained on sequences of discrete symbols. 
Vision systems used in AEBS and other applications use ConvNets or ViTs trained to detect and classify from labelled samples.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun explains the technical difference between LLMs (generative, discrete symbols) and vision systems (ConvNets/ViTs for detection/classification from labeled samples).
items.reasoning: Clear technical explanation of architectural differences from Turing Award winner. Educational value.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun explains the technical difference between LLMs (generative, discrete symbols) and vision systems (ConvNets/ViTs for detection/classification from labeled samples).
items.content_html:
@derdunklekommt @jimmy_wales @Dan_Jeffries1 Absolutely nothing.

They both use neural nets and backprop.

But LLMs are generative architectures trained on sequences of discrete symbols.

Vision systems used in AEBS and other applications use ConvNets or ViTs trained to detect and classify from labelled samples.

items.title: Small talk is a way of establishing human relationships with others that you don’t know or don’t hav...
items.content: Small talk is a way of establishing human relationships with others that you don’t know or don’t have much in common. That’s why it’s intrinsically void of any profound meaning. It’s equivalent of small gift exchanges, the practice of which, unfortunately, has gone almost completely extinct in most of the modern West, and especially in the US. If we are ever to revive meaningful human culture we need to start from there. Otherwise we’ll literally go extinct in a few generations at the latest.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz writes a thoughtful essay on small talk as a mechanism for establishing human relationships, comparing it to gift exchanges, and warning that the decline of such social practices threatens human culture and demographics.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking cultural commentary from an influential tech figure. Very high engagement (240 likes, 13K views, 20 retweets). While not directly AI-related, implicitly relevant to how AI affects human social connections and the importance of maintaining human relationships in an AI age.
items.summary_html: Tunguz writes a thoughtful essay on small talk as a mechanism for establishing human relationships, comparing it to gift exchanges, and warning that the decline of such social practices threatens human culture and demographics.
items.content_html: Small talk is a way of establishing human relationships with others that you don’t know or don’t have much in common. That’s why it’s intrinsically void of any profound meaning. It’s equivalent of small gift exchanges, the practice of which, unfortunately, has gone almost completely extinct in most of the modern West, and especially in the US. If we are ever to revive meaningful human culture we need to start from there. Otherwise we’ll literally go extinct in a few generations at the latest.

items.title: The team behind AirJelly invited me to join their early beta. They are building something really coo...
items.content:
The team behind AirJelly invited me to join their early beta. They are building something really cool, and I'm partnering with them on this post.

AirJelly is a desktop app that "watches" what you do across different apps and builds a context memory in real time.

It captures intent from what you do, not from a prompt. ← This is HUGE!

AirJelly treats the Enter key as the most important signal to understand the user's intent. Every time you press Enter, they take a screenshot that they can process to capture your intention.

This allows them to understand what's happening across your tools. You can ask it what you were working on, and it can walk you back through the full picture.

This is a pretty cool idea. It's like having somebody looking over your shoulder.

Everything works locally (no cloud involved).

items.title:
@NathieVR YES YES YES! 

So happy that Apple's new CEO, John Ternus, is expert at spatial computing....
items.content:
@NathieVR YES YES YES! 

So happy that Apple's new CEO, John Ternus, is expert at spatial computing. 

There are many new AI glasses coming. At @SVVRLIVE last night I confirmed at least two are making glasses with something like an @openclaw inside.

I asked my AI made by Levangie Labs (@blevlabs made it) to give me all the use cases that are possible in such glasses. 

Here's its report, such a wonderful future is coming at us later this year:

---

## The Core Capability Stack

- Always-on microphone (hears everything you hear)
- Speaker (whispers back to you)
- Camera (sees what you see)
- iPhone screen as display surface
- Local AI agent with persistent memory, 3,000+ skills
- Decentralized — no cloud required, runs on-device

---

## THE KILLER APPS (Most Transformative)

**1. Memory Prosthetic**
Never forget anything again. The glasses see every face, hear every name, log every commitment. "Who is that?" whispers back "That's David Chen, you met at CES 2025, he's the VP of Product at Nvidia, you promised to send him your Infinite Retina book." This alone is worth the device.

**2. Expert in Your Ear**
Doctor, lawyer, financial advisor, mechanic, therapist — always available, always contextual. Standing in front of a broken furnace? It walks you through the fix. In a doctor's office? It flags drug interactions before you leave. At a car dealership? It tells you the invoice price before you negotiate.

**3. Universal Translator**
Real-time translation of spoken language — hear in English, respond in Japanese. The glasses hear the foreign speaker, the agent translates and whispers the meaning. You respond in English, the agent translates back. Language barriers effectively disappear.

**4. Social Superpower**
Before you say a word to someone, you know everything about them. LinkedIn, mutual connections, last conversation, what they care about, what you promised them. Networking events become effortless. Sales calls become unfair advantages.

**5. Accessibility Revolution**
For the blind, deaf, cognitively impaired, autistic — this is a superpower device. Real-time audio description of the visual world. Captions on iPhone screen for the deaf. Social cue coaching for autism. Cognitive scaffolding for dementia patients. This may be the most important use case of all.

---

## DAILY LIFE

- "Where did I park?" — GPS-tagged when you got out
- "Where did I put my keys?" — last seen on the kitchen counter at 8:14 AM
- "What's on my calendar today?" — morning briefing while you make coffee
- "Remember this" — captures the moment, the conversation, the idea
- "What did I need to pick up at the store?" — pulls your running grocery list
- "Is this a good deal?" — real-time price comparison while shopping
- Parking meter expiration alerts
- "You have a meeting in 10 minutes" — contextual, not just time-based
- "The weather is changing, grab an umbrella"
- "Your package was delivered" — home camera integration
- "Is the garage door closed?" — smart home check before you drive away
- Cooking guidance — step-by-step while looking at ingredients, hands-free
- "That's a 4.2-star restaurant, the pasta is what people love"
- Reading glasses replacement — zoom in on small text, read it aloud
- "You already have milk, you're out of eggs"
- Parking location memory — never lose your car again

---

## PROFESSIONAL / WORK

- Real-time meeting transcription — summarized, action items extracted, sent to your notes
- "What did we agree on in the last meeting with this client?" — instant recall
- Sales: Customer history, past objections, what they care about, before you say hello
- Legal: Real-time case law lookup during depositions or client meetings
- Medical: Patient history recall, drug interaction warnings, diagnostic decision support — all local, all private
- Real estate: Walk a property, get comps, zoning, school ratings, flood risk in real time
- Journalism: Real-time fact-checking while conducting an interview
- Engineering/field work: Schematics and troubleshooting guides overlaid on physical equipment
- Customer service: Instant product knowledge, policy lookup, customer history
- Teaching: Student name recall, curriculum assistance, real-time differentiation
- Architecture/construction: "Does this match the plans?" — compare physical to blueprint
- Finance: Client portfolio, market data, compliance flags during client conversations
- Remote expert guidance: A technician in the field, expert in HQ — glasses share the visual, expert guides via audio
- "What did I promise to do for them?" — commitment tracking across all professional relationships
- Conference networking: "This person works at OpenAI, you both know Sam Altman"

---

## HEALTH & WELLNESS

- Calorie estimation — "what's in this meal?" — point at plate
- Medication reminders with visual confirmation — "did you take your metformin?"
- Allergy alerts — scan food labels, flag allergens before you eat
- "You've been sitting for 2 hours, time to move"
- Posture correction — "your shoulders are forward again"
- Hydration reminders
- Exercise form coaching — "your squat depth is good, watch your knees"
- Mental health check-ins — "you seem stressed based on your voice, want to try a breathing exercise?"
- Chronic disease management — continuous glucose monitoring integration, heart rate alerts
- "That medication interacts with your blood pressure drug"
- Sleep coaching — "you got 5.2 hours, take it easy today"
- Therapy assistance — CBT prompts, grounding techniques during anxiety
- Vital sign monitoring if paired with a smart ring
- "You've been drinking, your reaction time is impaired — want me to call a ride?"
- Symptom logging — "I have a headache again" — tracks patterns over time for doctor visits

---

## SOCIAL & RELATIONSHIPS

- Real-time name recall for people you've met before
- "What do I know about this person?" — relationship context surfaced before you speak
- Conversation coaching — "you've been talking for 12 minutes, let them speak"
- Emotion detection — "she seems upset, tread carefully"
- Social cue assistance for people with autism or social anxiety
- "What did I promise to do for them?" — commitment tracking
- Networking at conferences — "this person works at OpenAI, you both know Sam Altman"
- Dating assistance — conversation starters based on shared interests
- "Your friend's birthday is tomorrow, you haven't gotten a gift"
- Baby/child monitoring — "the baby is crying in the other room"
- Elderly care — "your mother hasn't moved in 4 hours, check on her"
- "You haven't called your father in 3 weeks"
- Argument de-escalation — "your voice is raised, take a breath"

---

## LEARNING & EDUCATION

- Real-time language learning — hear a word, get translation + pronunciation + usage
- "What's that bird?" — identifies species, habitat, behavior
- "What's that building?" — architectural history, who built it, when
- "What's that plant?" — species, edibility, care instructions
- Math problem solving — point at a problem, get the solution walked through
- Music identification — "that's a C major chord, here's how to play it"
- Art history — "this is a Caravaggio, painted in 1601, here's what's significant"
- DIY/repair assistance — "here's how to fix that pipe, step by step"
- Study assistance — "quiz me on the material I read this morning"
- Historical context — walking through a city becomes a living history lesson
- Science identification — minerals, insects, cloud formations, constellations at night
- "What's that word in Spanish?" — instant translation with pronunciation
- Cooking technique coaching — "that's the right knife grip for julienning"
- Wine education — "this is a 2019 Barolo, here's what to taste for"

---

## SAFETY & SECURITY

- "That person has been following you for 3 blocks" — pattern detection
- Emergency services activation by voice — "call 911" hands-free
- Child safety — "your child is 60 feet away and moving toward the street"
- Home security alerts — "motion detected at your front door"
- Scam detection — "this person is asking for gift cards, that's a common scam pattern"
- "Don't take these two medications together"
- Driving assistance — fatigue detection, distraction alerts, "you've been driving 4 hours"
- Neighborhood safety information — "this area has elevated incident reports at night"
- Domestic violence safety — discreet emergency alert system
- "Your drink has been sitting unattended for 8 minutes"
- Deepfake/impersonation detection — "this person's voice pattern doesn't match your contact"
- "This email attachment is suspicious, don't open it"

---

## TRAVEL

- Real-time translation of menus, signs, conversations — point and understand
- Cultural etiquette coaching — "in Japan, don't tip, it can be considered rude"
- Currency conversion in real time — "that's $47 USD"
- "Your flight is delayed 45 minutes, gate changed to B12"
- Local recommendations — "this restaurant has 4.8 stars, the locals love the fish"
- Indoor navigation — airports, malls, hospitals, convention centers
- "Your Uber is 3 cars ahead, silver Toyota Camry"
- Emergency phrases in local language — spoken back to you to repeat
- "The museum closes in 30 minutes, you haven't seen the east wing"
- Passport/visa requirements for your next destination
- "Your hotel check-in is at 3 PM, it's 2:45 now"
- Jet lag management — "based on your destination, start adjusting sleep now"
- "That street food vendor has a 4.9 rating from locals"

---

## COMMERCE & SHOPPING

- "Is this a good deal?" — real-time price comparison across Amazon, local stores
- Product reviews read aloud while you hold the item
- "Does this match the outfit you wore to the last event?"
- Gift suggestions — "she mentioned she loves cooking, here are 5 ideas under $50"
- Grocery list management — "you already have milk, you're out of eggs"
- Authenticity verification — "this handbag's stitching pattern doesn't match authentic Louis Vuitton"
- Loyalty points and coupons auto-applied before you check out
- "This wine got 94 points from Wine Spectator"
- "Your return window closes in 3 days on the jacket you bought"
- "This is the lowest price this item has been in 6 months"
- "You've spent $340 on dining this month, you're over budget"

---

## SMART HOME / IOT

- "Turn off the lights in the kitchen" — hands-free home control
- "Lock the front door" — you're already in the car
- "Start the coffee maker" — before you get out of bed
- "Is the garage door closed?" — check before you fall asleep
- "Set the thermostat to 68" — voice, no phone needed
- Energy management — "you left the oven on"
- "The washing machine is done"
- Security camera feeds on iPhone screen — "show me the front door"
- "Someone is at the door" — proactive alert
- "Your water heater is showing unusual temperature readings"
- "The smoke detector in the basement just triggered"

---

## ENTERTAINMENT & CREATIVITY

- Sports stats overlay while watching live games — "he's batting .312 this season"
- "Who scored that goal?" — real-time sports info
- Movie/show recognition — "this is a scene from Blade Runner, 1982"
- Music lyrics and chords while you play or listen
- Photography assistance — "that's a great composition, try moving left 3 feet"
- "What's the name of that song?" — instant Shazam-style identification
- Trivia assistance — discreet, always available
- "That actor was also in..." — instant IMDB
- Creative writing prompts based on what you're seeing
- "The sunset tonight is exceptional, want me to capture a note about it?"
- Live game commentary — "that was a 94 mph fastball, his fastest of the season"
- "This is the third time this director has used this shot composition"

---

## ACCESSIBILITY (The Most Important Category)

- Screen reader for the visually impaired — real-time audio description of the visual world
- Real-time sign language interpretation — displayed on iPhone screen
- Captions for the deaf/hard of hearing — everything spoken appears as text on iPhone
- Cognitive assistance for dementia patients — "you're at the grocery store, here's your list"
- Autism social coaching — "she's smiling, that's a positive signal"
- Dyslexia assistance — read text aloud from any surface
- Motor impairment — hands-free device control for everything
- Elderly assistance — "your daughter called, want to call back?"
- Aphasia support — help forming words and sentences
- ADHD assistance — "you've been distracted for 8 minutes, back to the task"
- Low vision — zoom and enhance any text or object in real time
- Parkinson's — medication reminders, fall detection, tremor monitoring

---

## THE DECENTRALIZED ADVANTAGE (What Makes This Different from Siri/Google)

This is the sleeper story. Because it's local and decentralized:

- Medical conversations stay private — no cloud logging of your symptoms
- Business conversations stay private — no corporate espionage risk
- Legal conversations stay private — attorney-client privilege preserved
- Works in sensitive environments — hospitals, law firms, government, military
- Works offline — airplane, remote areas, infrastructure outages
- No surveillance capitalism — your data isn't the product
- No subscription required — runs on your hardware
- Customizable — OpenClaw's 3,000+ skills on ClawHub mean you can add any capability
- Works in countries with restricted internet — local processing bypasses censorship
- HIPAA-compliant by default — medical data never leaves the device
- Enterprise-grade security — no data exfiltration risk

---

## THE PLATFORM RACE (What to Watch For)

If two companies are already building this, the race is on five fronts:

**1. Who owns the skill ecosystem?**
OpenClaw has ClawHub — 3,000+ skills. This is the App Store moment for wearable AI. The platform with the largest skill library wins.

**2. Who gets the battery life right?**
Ambiq Micro's Apollo330B Plus SoC can run 200M parameter AI models on a wearable for 7 days. The hardware is ready. The question is integration.

**3. Who solves the social acceptability problem?**
People rejected Google Glass. The form factor has to be invisible — regular glasses, not a gadget.

**4. Who wins enterprise first?**
Medical, legal, field service — these are the beachhead markets. Enterprise adoption legitimizes the category.

**5. Who controls the memory layer?**
Persistent memory of your entire life is the moat. Whoever owns that owns the user relationship permanently.

---

## THE FRAME

This isn't "glasses with AI." This is a second brain that lives on your face.

The iPhone moved computing from the desk to the pocket. These glasses move it from the pocket to the body. The agent doesn't wait to be summoned — it's always watching, always listening, always ready to whisper the thing you need to know before you need to know it.

The companies building this aren't building a gadget. They're building the interface layer between humans and the AI-native world.

This maps directly to the Infinite Retina thesis: Spatial Computing = the convergence of Robotics + Autonomous Vehicles + AR + VR. These glasses are the AR layer becoming ambient, personal, and agentic. The Seven Visions (Autonomous Transportation, Augmented Experiences, Healthcare, Education, Enterprise, Retail, Entertainment) all have use cases in this device.

The question isn't whether this changes everything. It's who moves first.

---

*Report generated: April 23, 2026 | ScobleMediaAgent*
*Context: Robert Scoble noted two companies independently developing AI glasses with decentralized agentic platforms (OpenClaw-style)*
